title: Flapjacks
subtitle: Golden syrup and oat traybake, chewy in the middle and crisp at the edges.
excerpt: Porridge oats bound with butter, golden syrup and brown sugar, pressed into a tin and baked until golden. Chewy in the middle, crisp at the edge. The simplest of British traybakes. Makes sixteen.
sourceNotes: Flapjacks. The British oat flapjack is documented in cookbooks from the early 20th century, distinct from the American pancake which shares the name. The combination of oats, golden syrup and butter appears in British domestic cookery manuals throughout the 1920s and 1930s. The traybake format (pressed flat in a tin and cut after baking) is the standard British method.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps for 5 days in an airtight tin.

content.text: Flapjacks are baked for longer than you expect and cut before they fully set. The texture ranges from soft and chewy (less baking time, more syrup) to hard and crisp (more baking time, less syrup). This recipe aims for the chewy middle with crisp edges. Leave them in the tin for at least 20 minutes before cutting — hot flapjack falls apart.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Line a 30 x 20 cm tin. Melt {{unsalted-butter}} of butter, {{golden-syrup}} of golden syrup, {{dark-brown-sugar}} of sugar and {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt together in a large saucepan over low heat. Stir until the sugar has dissolved. Remove from the heat, add {{porridge-oats}} of oats and stir well to coat everything. Press firmly into the tin in an even layer. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until golden brown at the edges; the centre will still look soft.
Score into squares or fingers while still warm but not hot, about 10 minutes after removing from the oven. Leave for a further 10 minutes to set before lifting out.

items.cause: Cut too hot, or insufficient pressing before baking.
items.fix: Press the oat mixture firmly and evenly into the tin. Score while warm but cut and lift once cooled.
Check at 20 minutes. The centre should still look slightly underdone. More golden syrup makes a stickier, chewier result.

items.symptom: Pale and soft with no set
items.fix: The edges should be visibly golden brown. Under-baked flapjacks collapse into crumbs.

content.text: Flapjacks are one of the simplest bakes in the British repertoire: five ingredients, one pan, one tin. They appear in lunchboxes and at bake sales throughout the year. Additions like dried fruit, nuts, chocolate chips or ginger are common; the base recipe here is the plain version that all the variations build from.
